coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots coordinated to mercaptopyridine-substituted phthalocyanines: Characterization and application as fluorescence “turn ON” nanoprobes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: This study reports on the design of novel nanoconjugates of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and tetra or octa-mercaptopyridine-substituted zinc and aluminium phthalocyanines (Pcs) deployed as fluorescence “turn ON” nanoprobes. The phthalocyanines were separately adsorbed onto the planar structure of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via π-π stacking interaction to form GQDs-mercaptopyridine Pcs nanoconjugates. The quaternized Pc complexes could also interact with the GQDs through electrostatic attraction due to the positive charges on the Pcs ring substituents and the negative charges on the surface of GQDs. The fluorescence emission of the GQDs was quenched upon coordination to the respective Pcs. However, the fluorescence emission was “turned ON” in the presence of Hg2+ employed as a test analyte. The mechanism of the “turn ON” of the GQDs emission in the nanoconjugates is ascribed to the strong affinity of Hg2+ to bind with the bridging sulfur on the Pcs periphery thereby disrupting the π-π stacking interaction between the GQDs and the Pcs with a consequent “turn ON” of the coordinated GQDs' fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Mercaptopyridine phthalocyanines
Mercury (II) ion
Fluorescence ‘turn-ON’

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Coordination of GQDs to Mercaptopyridine-substituted Pcs
Procedure for the Optical Detection of Hg2+
Characterization of the Nanoconjugates of GQDs and Mercaptopyridine-substituted Pcs
TEM Images and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)
Applications of the Nanoconjugates for Analyte Sensing
Fluorescence “Turn ON” of the Nanoconjugates in the Presence of Hg2+
Proposed Mechanism for the Novel Detection Systems

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular structures of zinc tetra-substituted 4-(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyanine and its quaternized version (1 and 2). Quaternized zinc octa-substituted 4-(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyanine (3) and quaternized aluminium tetra-substituted 4-(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyanine (4).
FT-IR spectra of GQDs (a) 2, (b) and GQDs-2 (c).
TEM images (top) and DLS graphs (bottom) of GQDs (A and B), GQDs-1 (C and D) and GQDs-2 (E and F).
Ground state absorption (a) and emission (b) spectra of GQDs at 340nm excitation wavelength. Solvent=PBS (10mM). Insets: GQDs solution viewed under UV (365nm) and visible light.
UV/vis absorption spectra of complexes 1, 2, 3 or 4 in DMF.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of (A) 1, (B) 2, (C) 3 and (D) 4. (a) Pc alone in PBS, (b) GQDs-Pcs in PBS only and (c) GQDs-Pcs in PBS plus 4% DMF.
Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs alone and the nanoconjugates. Solvent: PBS (10mM, pH7.4). λexc =340nm.
Overlap between the emission spectrum of GQDs and the absorption spectra of complexes 3 and 4. λexc =340nm. Solvent: PBS (10mM, pH7.4).

Fluorescence emission spectra of 0.5mg/mL (A) GQDs-2 and (B) GQDs-3, as examples in PBS (10mM, pH7.4) upon addition of varying concentration of Hg2+ (0.5–50nM). Insets: Calibration curves of F/F0 against Hg2+ concentration [Hg2+]. λexc =340nm. Number of replicate measurements, n =3.

Selective screening of different metal ions and their effects on the fluorescence response of the nanoprobes of 0.5mg/mL GQDs-2 and GQDs-3. [Hg2+]=50nM. Concentration of interfering ions=500nM. Solvent=PBS (10mM, pH7.4). Number of replicate measurements, n =3. Tolerable error limits=±5%.
Overlay of the fluorescence lifetime curves of GQDs (alone) and GQDs-2 (as a representative) in the absence and presence of 1.0×10−10 M of Hg2+ in PBS (10mM, pH7.4).
Schematic illustration of the mechanism for Hg2+ detection using the nanoconjugates of GQDs and mercaptopyridine-substituted Pcs.
Fluorescence quantum yields, FRET efficiencies, fluorescence lifetimes and Stern-Volmer constant for GQDs-Pc conjugates. Solvent PBS (10mM, pH7.4), unless otherwise stated.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The monomer peak of Pc in the conjugate with GQDs (all Pc-GQDs aggregated).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets for PBS plus 4% DMF.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The comparison of LODs of the present method and reported methods on GQDs-based nanoprobes for the detection of Hg2+.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in bracket are recorded in the presence of 4% DMF addition to PBS (10mM, pH7.4).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots coordinated to mercaptopyridine-substituted phthalocyanines: Characterization and application as fluorescence “turn ON” nanoprobes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports on the design of novel nanoconjugates of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and tetra or octa-mercaptopyridine-substituted zinc and aluminium phthalocyanines (Pcs) deployed as fluorescence “turn ON” nanoprobes. The phthalocyanines were separately adsorbed onto the planar structure of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via π-π stacking interaction to form GQDs-mercaptopyridine Pcs nanoconjugates. The quaternized Pc complexes could also interact with the GQDs through electrostatic attraction due to the positive charges on the Pcs ring substituents and the negative charges on the surface of GQDs. The fluorescence emission of the GQDs was quenched upon coordination to the respective Pcs. However, the fluorescence emission was “turned ON” in the presence of Hg2+ employed as a test analyte. The mechanism of the “turn ON” of the GQDs emission in the nanoconjugates is ascribed to the strong affinity of Hg2+ to bind with the bridging sulfur on the Pcs periphery thereby disrupting the π-π stacking interaction between the GQDs and the Pcs with a consequent “turn ON” of the coordinated GQDs' fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials characterized by tunable photoluminescence (PL), good electron donating abilities, robust chemical inertness, excellent photostability, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high solubility in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents [1,2]. GQDs have emerged as novel materials for the fabrication and design of nanosensors and are effective alternatives to semiconductor QDs [3]. GQDs have been employed in the detection of various target analytes ranging from metal ions, DNA, proteins, small organic molecules and biomaterials with comparatively better biocompatibility, sensitivity and selectivity than semiconductor QDs, metallic nanoparticles (Au and Ag) or carbon nanotubes [4]. The direct deployment of GQDs alone for analytes sensing generally involves the quenching (“turn OFF”) of GQDs fluorescence emission which is less reliable since the quenching may not be due to the analytes alone, thereby resulting in false positive signals. Conversely, fluorescence “turn ON” approach is specific and more reliable. The controlled functionalization of GQDs with biomolecules and macrocycles has resulted in improved photophysical and chemical properties, leading to various applications of GQDs such as in nanoprobe fabrication (mostly as “turn ON” sensors), fluorescence imaging microscopy and photocatalysis [4–6]. In light of the above, the nanoconjugates of GQDs and macrocycles with extended π-conjugated systems such as phthalocyanines (Pcs) could considerably extend GQDs-based nanosensors fabrication. GQDs' planar structure consisting of delocalized π electrons of graphene enables strong π-π interactions with other π-conjugated aromatic molecules [7]. The presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on GQDs surface and edges enable covalent attachment, electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding with other suitable moieties [8]. The synthetic versatility and excellent optical properties of phthalocyanines and porphyrins (Pcs) [9,10] have motivated their deployment in the fabrication of multifunctional hybrid materials in ‘donor–acceptor’ supramolecular ensembles involving various carbon-based nanomaterials, QDs and metallic nanoparticles [11–13]. GQDs assembled with metalloporphyrins (similar in structure to phthalocyanines) have been deployed for hydrogen peroxide and glucose sensing via a fluorescence “turn ON” process [14]. The GQDs fluorescence emission was quenched by the metalloporphyrin and was ‘switched ON’ in the presence of hydrogen peroxide [14]. The quenching of GQDs fluorescence by ZnPc complexes upon coordination was reported as well by us, but the quenching was not used for analyte detection [15].

Despite the potential of GQDs, not much is known about their conjugates with macrocycles possessing excellent electronic and optical properties such as phthalocyanines and porphyrins, hence limited investigations as probe materials. As such, much work is still required to fully exploit GQDs conjugates with macrocycles in nanosensors development which is the focus of this study.

The phthalocyanines employed and comparatively assessed in this study are: 2,3-tetra-(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato zinc (II) (1), quaternized 2,3-tetra-[(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato] zinc(II) (2), quaternized 2,3-octakis-[(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato] zinc(II) (3), quaternized 2,3-tetra-(2-mercaptopyridine)phthalocyaninato chloro aluminium (III) (4) (Fig. 1 ). The Pcs were non-covalently coordinated to the GQDs through π-π bonding with the possibility of additional electrostatic interactions for complexes 2, 3 or 4 due to the presence of negative charges on the GQDs surface and positive charges in 2, 3 and 4 due to quaternization. The emission of the GQDs was quenched (‘turned OFF’) upon coordination to the Pcs. The quenched emission of the GQDs was gradually recovered upon the addition of increasing concentrations of Hg2+ due to strong affinity and binding of the test analyte (Hg2+) to the bridging sulfur on the Pcs. The novel nanoprobes were selectively screened in the presence of various cations and showed high sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg2+ detection based on ‘turn ON’ of the GQDs' emission. We compare the effect of octa-substitution in 3 with tetra-substitution in 2; the effect of quaternization (comparing 1 and 2) and the effects of central metal (comparing 2 with 4).

Natural graphite powder (<20μm), Rhodamine 6G, mercuric acetate, and dialysis membrane tubing (MWCO 1.5kDa) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water obtained from a Mili-Q Water system (Millipore Corp. Bedford, MA, USA). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 10mM, pH7.4) was employed for all aqueous solutions. All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received. The phthalocyanines: 2,3-tetra-(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato zinc (II) (1), quaternized 2,3-tetra-[(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato] zinc(II) (2), quaternized 2,3-octakis-[(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato] zinc(II) (3), quaternized 2,3-tetra-(2-mercaptopyridine) phthalocyaninato chloro aluminium (III) (4) were synthesized according to previously reported procedures [16–18]. GQDs were synthesized by the top-down hydrothermal method as reported elsewhere [15].

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. Ground state electronic absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the range 300–800nm. Infra-red spectra were collected on a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with platinum-ATR. A Metrohm Swiss 827 pH meter was used for pH measurements.

Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 200, Picoquant GmbH) with a diode laser as described previously [19]. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80kV.
Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coordination of GQDs to Mercaptopyridine-substituted Pcs
ce:section.ce:para: The coordination of GQDs to complexes 1, 2, 3 or 4 was achieved following published procedures [15,20] but with slight modification. Briefly: GQDs (2mg/mL in 10mM PBS) were added to 0.2mg of complexes 1, 2, 3 and 4 in 5mL of dry DMF. Following optimization studies, this ratio was found to be optimal for the quenching of the fluorescence of the Pc complexes.

The mixtures of GQDs and the respective Pcs were ultrasonicated for 4h, followed by stirring in the dark for 4days. The green color of the Pcs turned light green due to adsorption to the GQDs. Ethanol was used to repeatedly wash the products. The solid products were dried under vacuum overnight at 60°C. The obtained nanoconjugates are represented as GQDs-1, GQDs-2, GQDs-3 and GQDs-4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the Optical Detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized nanoconjugates: GQDs-1, GQDs-2, GQDs-3 or GQDs-4 (0.1mg) were separately dissolved in PBS (10mM, pH7.4) containing 4% (v/v) DMF and ultrasonicated for 2min to ensure proper dispersion. This was followed by the addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ aqueous solution (2.5–50nM) sequentially at fixed intervals. Stable fluorescence signals were obtained by measuring the fluorescence emission of the resulting mixtures after 2min of equilibration time. An excitation wavelength of 340nm was employed.
Fluorescence quantum yields of the GQDs were determined by the comparative methods reported in literature [21], using Rhodamine 6G in ethanol (ΦF =0.95 [22]) as a standard.

GQDs fluorescence quantum yields after conjugation with the respective Pcs
Φ
F
GQDs
Conjugate
were determined using Eq. (1):
(1)
Φ
F
GQDs
Conjugate
=
Φ
F
GQDs
F Conjugate
F
GQDs
where
Φ
F
GQDs
(fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs alone) was used as the standard, F(GQDs) is the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs alone and Fconjugate is the fluorescence intensities of the GQDs coordinated to the respective Pcs at the excitation of wavelength of 340nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the Nanoconjugates of GQDs and Mercaptopyridine-substituted Pcs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis and characterization (XPS and Raman) of the GQDs alone employed in this study have been reported [15] (See Supporting information, Fig. S1 and S2 for XPS and Raman spectroscopic analysis).

As stated above, the nanoconjugates of GQDs and the Pcs were formed by π-π stacking interactions by taking the advantage of the presence of delocalized π-electron systems in the GQDs and the Pcs. Electrostatic interactions are also likely to occur between the GQDs and the quaternized Pcs (2, 3 or 4). Non-covalent methods of fabricating graphene-based nanomaterials/supramolecular assemblies involving π-π interaction are advantageous in the sense that electronic properties of the nanomaterials are preserved [23], hence were adopted to obtain the GQDs-Pcs nanoconjugates in this work.

The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs alone (Fig. 2(a)) shows a broad absorption band from 3566 to 3019cm−1 which is a characteristic absorption band for OH stretching vibration. The peak occurring around 1542cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the carboxylic group present on the edges of the GQDs.

FT-IR spectra of 2 and GQDs-2 (as examples) are shown in Fig. 2b and c, respectively. There is a shift in the broad absorption found around 3405–3092cm−1 in GQDs-2 compared to that of the GQDs alone (at 3566–3019cm−1). A vibration recorded at 1555cm−1 is attributed to the C C stretching vibration in GQDs-2 which is similarly present in 2 (Fig. 2b) with a shift to 1563cm−1. Comparing the spectrum of GQDs-2 (Fig. 2c) to that of 2 alone (Fig. 2b) and GQDs alone (Fig. 2a), it is clearly evident that the nanoconjugates showed the respective peaks corresponding to both GQDs and the Pcs with some peak shifts, further indicating the formation of new nanocomplexes. It is known that shifts in IR bands confirm structural changes [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM Images and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image recorded for the GQDs alone (Fig. 3A) and the GQDs-Pcs nanoprobes (Fig. 3C and E) showed aggregation which was more enhanced in conjugates. The size of the GQDs alone was estimated to be 4nm.

Dynamic light scattering measurements showed that the size of GQDs alone was 6.5nm (Fig. 3B) which is slightly higher than the average size of 4nm obtained using TEM (Fig. 3A). DLS experiments indicate an increase in size of the nanoconjugates (GQDs-1 and GQDs-2 in Fig. 3D and F) as compared to the GQDs alone (Fig. 3A). The average sizes of the nanoconjugates were found to be 14, 21, 27 and 22nm for GQDs-1, GQDs-2, GQDs-3, and GQDs-4, respectively. The increase in the size of the nanoconjugates compared to GQDs alone is most likely due to aggregation. Aggregation may occur through π-π stacking of the Pcs on adjacent GQDs. GQDs-3 gave the largest size which suggests more aggregation and/or coordination. The increased aggregation for GQDs-3 could be as a result of the presence of more delocalized π electrons due to the octa substitution, which overcomes the effect of ring substituents in preventing aggregation.

The spectra were also acquired in PBS in order to allow an aqueous medium for Hg2+ detection. Complexes 2, 3 and 4 are water soluble, with 3 showing more aqueous solubility due to the quaternized octa-substituents. Complexes 2, 3 and 4 were aggregated in PBS (10mM pH7.4), Fig. 6B–D (curves a). Aggregation in Pcs is judged by broadened or split Q bands in which the high energy band is due to the aggregate (the so called “H” aggregate) and the low energy band is due to the monomer [9]. Although, complex 1 alone is not soluble in aqueous medium, its conjugate with GQDs could be dispersed in aqueous PBS solution. Aggregation was observed in all the nanoconjugates in PBS, Fig. 6A–D (curves b). The absorption occurring below 600nm for the nanoconjugates is due to the intense absorption of GQDs in this region. Fig. 6 shows that in PBS (for complexes 2 and 3), there were red shifts in the Q bands (the monomer peaks) upon conjugation. Red shifts and flattening of similar macrocycles such as porphyrins have been observed in porphyrins-graphene oxide nanoconjugates [28].

In order to reduce the aggregation in aqueous medium, a small amount of DMF (~ 4%) was added to the PBS solutions of the nanoconjugates and aggregation was reduced considerably in GQDs-2 and GQDs-3, but not for GQDs-1 and GQDs-4, Fig. 6A–D (curves c). Organic solvents are known to reduce aggregation in phthalocyanines [9].

GQDs showed excitation wavelength-independent emissions with no changes in the spectral width or emission wavelength maxima at different excitation wavelengths. Maximum emission intensity was at 445nm (Fig. 4b). The GQDs showed bright blue emission under 365nm UV (Fig. 4 inset). This emission has been ascribed to the presence of surface functional groups (OH and COOH) which act as radiative recombination centres promoting the relaxation of excited electrons within the GQDs nanoparticles in addition to GQDs intrinsic emissive sites which is still under debate [29]. The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) and lifetimes (τF) of GQDs alone were measured to be 0.22 and 5.70 (ns), respectively (Table 1).

The fluorescence emission spectra of the GQDs upon coordination to the respective Pcs (1–4) indicated that the GQDs fluorescence was quenched in the presence of the respective Pcs within the nanoconjugates when compared to the GQDs alone (Fig. 7 ) followed by stimulated emissions in the Pcs (Fig. S3, GQDs-2, shown as an example). Studies have shown that GQDs are excellent energy and electron donors in ‘host-guest’ interactions involving biomolecules and aromatic macromolecules leading to the depopulation of their excited state and the quenching of their fluorescence emission [7,30,31]. This phenomenon was observed in this study upon the coordination of GQDs to the mercaptopyridine-substituted Pcs (1, 2, 3 or 4). The largest decrease in fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) was for GQDs-3 and the lowest was observed in GQDs-1. As stated above, complexes 2, 3 or 4 could also interact with the GQDs by electrostatic interaction, while for GQDs-1 only π-π interaction occurred (since it was not quaternized). The stronger interaction of the GQDs with complex 3 could be ascribed to the presence of more positively charged substituents (octa-substitution as compared to the tetra-substitution in 2 or 4), resulting in higher quenching of the GQDs fluorescence. The trends in ΦF values in the presence of 4% DMF was the same as in PBS alone. The fluorescence lifetimes also become shorter on conjugation of GQDs with complexes 1, 2, 3 or 4, with 3 exhibiting the shortest lifetime corresponding to the lowest fluorescence quantum yield in the GQDs-3 nanoconjugate (Table 1).

To better understand the extent of the interaction between GQDs and the Pcs, the emission spectrum of GQDs was overlayed with the absorption spectra of complexes 3 or 4 (Fig. 8 ). Even though the spectral overlaps were minimal, a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism could still be at play in the nanoconjugates. Similar mechanism has been widely reported in nanoconjugates of semiconductor QDs and Pcs, where energy is transferred from QDs to Pcs macrocycles leading to the quenching of emission of the former [32]. FRET efficiency (Eff) was estimated experimentally from the fluorescence quantum yields of the GQDs in the absence (ΦF(GQDs) ) and presence
Φ
F
GQDs
Conjugate
of the Pcs using Eq. (2) [33]:
(2)
Eff = 1 −
Φ
F
GQDs
conjugate
Φ
F
GQDs

The Eff value was higher in GQDs-3 compared to the other nanoconjugates which further indicated that complex 3 interacted more closely with the GQDs than complexes 1, 2 or 4 (Table 1). As already stated, in addition to the π-π stacking coordination between GQDs and 2, 3 or 4, there is the possibility of electrostatic interactions due to the quaternized pyridinic nitrogens of the Pc complexes which could lead to enhanced coordination to the GQDs. However, the coordination of GQDs to complex 3 is envisaged to be more favoured electrostatically due to the presence of more positively charged substituents as mentioned above. It should be noted that the calculated FRET efficiency values are only estimates, since there are a wide variety of factors [34] which influence the decrease in QDs emission in addition to FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the Nanoconjugates for Analyte Sensing
Fluorescence “Turn ON” of the Nanoconjugates in the Presence of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission of GQDs upon coordination to the Pcs resulted in a “turn OFF” process (Fig. 7). The fluorescence was “turned ON” by Hg2+. Thus, the newly fabricated nanoprobes of GQDs-1, GQDs-2, GQDs-3 or GQDs-4 were deployed for the fluorimetric sensing of Hg2+ since only this analyte modulated the GQDs emission within the nanoconjugates. The enhancement (“turn ON”) of the GQDs fluorescence emission upon increasing the concentration of Hg2+ is shown in Fig. 9 A and B. The fluorescence emission wavelength maximum of the GQDs (alone) remained unchanged at the investigated concentration range of the test analyte which points to the fact that the “turn ON” signals are as a result of the binding of Hg2+ to the Pcs.

In order to evaluate the fluorescence “turn ON” sensitivity of the nanoprobes, the emission intensities of GQDs-1, GQDs-2, GQDs-3 or GQDs-4 in the absence (F0) and presence (F) of Hg2+ were evaluated using Eq. (3) [35].
(3)
F
F 0
= 1 + K
Hg
2 +
F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher, respectively. K is the fluorescence enhancement constant.

F/F0 was used due to fluorescence enhancement instead of F0/F normally used to indicate a fluorescence quenching process. As shown in the insets of Fig. 9, the plots of F/F0 against Hg2+ concentrations were linear. K values (Table 1) were used to assess the sensitivity of the nanoprobes. A higher value of K was obtained for GQDs-3 in the presence of the Hg2+ compared to GQDs-1, GQDs-2 or GQDs-4, which showed that GQDs-3 had better detection sensitivity for the target analyte and follows the order GQDs-3 >GQDs-2 >GQDs-4 >GQDs-1 (Table 1), for reasons explained above. The fluorescence signals increased linearly with the concentrations of Hg2+ in the range of 0.5–50nM. The limits of detection (LODs) were evaluated using the equation LOD=3 δ/K, where δ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n =10) and K is the slope of the calibration curves. The LODs obtained were 1.5nM for GQDs-1, 0.52nM for GQDs-2, 0.12nM for GQDs-3 and 0.60nM for GQDs-4 nanoprobes in PBS (Table 2 ).

The results obtained for GQDs-1, GQDs-2, GQDs-3 and GQDs-4 nanoprobes indicated that the nanoconjugates could function as excellent nanoprobes for the detection of Hg2+ and showed as well that complex 1 (unquaternized) could be employed as nanoprobe for Hg2+ sensing when coupled to GQDs but with a lower sensitivity as compared to complexes 2, 3 or 4 (which could be ascribed to their improved interaction with the GQDs due to quaternization and improved aqueous solubility). The low activity of complex 1 could be due to its low solubility in aqueous media compared to the rest of the complexes. Values in PBS plus 4% DMF (where there is less aggregation for 2 or 3) are similar to those in PBS alone where there is considerable aggregation for all the nanoconjugates, hence showing no effects of aggregation in the fluorescence “turn ON” signals as reflected in the obtained LOD values.

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time GQDs coupled with phthalocyanines (GQDs-Pcs conjugates) are used for Hg2+ detection via fluorescence “turn ON” mechanism. As such, we compared our LOD values with methods based on the application of GQDs alone or with Au nanoparticles sensing systems from the literature. Our nanoprobes showed LOD values within the nanomolar range (Table 2) [36–40] with GQDs-3 showing better values than those reported in the literature. Moreover, most methods reported in the literature involving the use of GQDs alone as nanosensors are based on “turn OFF” designs whereas our method is based on fluorescence “turn ON” process which is more reliable than the “turn-OFF” process (since “turn-OFF” may not be only due to the effects of the test analytes). Thus, this study presents more reliable sensing materials for Hg2+ detection and quantification utilizing GQDs conjugates with phthalocyanines.

It is noteworthy to state that the water soluble Pcs (2, 3 or 4) were coordinated to the GQDs and the resulting nanoconjugates used as nanoprobes, which implies that these nanoprobes could easily be deployed for the detection and quantification of Hg2+ in aqueous media which is highly desirable. Quaternized tetra-substituted ZnPc (2) proved to be a better nanoprobe compared to quaternized tetra-substituted AlPc (4) judging from their respective of limit of detection for Hg2+ sensing (Table 2). The nanoprobes responsiveness to the presence of Hg2+ remained unchanged after several months of fabrication which attests to the stability of the probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed Mechanism for the Novel Detection Systems
ce:section.ce:para: In order to elucidate the underlying mechanisms for the fluorescence “turn ON” of the GQDs emission in the presence of the test analyte, fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried out. As shown in Fig. 11 , the fluorescence decay curves of GQDs-2 exhibited an increase in the fluorescence decay lifetime in the presence of Hg2+ to a value closer to that of the GQDs alone, Fig. 11. Similar concentration of Hg2+ was added to the GQDs solution and there were no changes in the GQDs fluorescence lifetime. Since the GQDs emission was restored upon interaction of the nanoconjugates with Hg2+, the fluorescence principle of “off-on” mode could be proposed to have taken place. In all the nanoconjugates, we theorize that the quenching of the GQDs emission resulted when the GQDs transmitted their energy to the Pcs (1, 2, 3 or 4) leading to the deactivation of the radiative recombination pathways and the depopulation of the excited states of GQDs, Scheme 1 . Conversely, in the presence of Hg2+, the energy transfer process could have been disrupted due to the binding of the Hg2+ to complexes 1, 2, 3 or 4 and the GQDs emission “turned ON” as illustrated in Scheme 1.

The disruption of the energy transfer process in the GQDs and the Pcs nanoconjugates by Hg2+ is predicated on two reasons. Firstly, the coordinated Pcs possess sulfur bearing substituents which have specific and strong affinity to preferentially bind Hg2+. Thus, Hg2+ could bind specifically to the mercapto groups of the Pc complexes coordinated to the GQDs and potentially result in the fluorescence “turn ON” mode of the GQDs. Secondly, we theorize that Hg2+ could act as a spacer between the GQDs and the Pcs resulting in the disruption of the π-π interaction and the ‘donor-acceptor’ relationship between the GQDs and the Pcs. Hence GQDs fluorescence is “turned ON”.

To further buttress these points, ground state absorption spectra of the GQDs-Pcs conjugates were recorded upon addition of different concentrations of Hg2+ to the nanoprobes solutions. Marked changes in the absorption spectra of GQDs-3 (shown in Fig. S4 as an example) were observed as there was a red shift of the monomer component of the Q-band from 718 to 732nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+ (Fig. S4), respectively, which is an indication of binding interaction between the Pcs and the Hg2+ ions, hence the spectral shift. Red shifting is associated with the formation of the rare J aggregates (edge-to-edge as opposed to face-to-face H aggregates) in phthalocyanines [42]. The red shifting has been observed on coordination of metals on sulfur containing phthalocyanines [42] hence we suggest that the red shift observed in Fig. S4 is a result of the formation of these aggregates following coordination of Hg2+.

We have deployed for the first time, nanoconjugates of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and mercaptopyridine-substituted phthalocyanines (Pcs) as fluorescence “turn ON” nanoprobes. The GQDs fluorescence was quenched in the presence of the respective Pcs and was restored upon interaction with a test analyte (Hg2+). The fabricated nanoprobes exhibited superior sensing properties for Hg2+ than other reported GQDs based sensing platforms (which are predominantly “turn OFF” processes). High selectivity was achieved towards Hg2+ than the other metal ions screened. Results revealed that the quaternized octa-substituted Pc (3) upon coordination to GQDs could function as a better nanoprobe compared to the quaternized tetra-substituted derivatives (2 or 4). Also, quaternized tetra-substituted ZnPc (2) showed to be a better nanoprobe compared to quaternized tetra-substituted AlPc (4) for this particular study. Based on the high sensitivity and selectivity displayed by the nanoconjugates towards Hg2+ detection over other metal ions, nanoprobes with potential application for Hg2+ detection with lower detection limits have been developed in this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2016.11.043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Acta Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc. Spectrochim. Acta A
Decontamination of Heavy Metals: Processes, Mechanisms, and Applications
